title: 🟢 Arrays & HashMap — Dry Run & Recall
oneSentence: Trace Two Sum, Group Anagrams, Valid Sudoku, and Subarray Sum Equals K step-by-step.

twoSum.title: Two Sum
twoSum.description: Find two numbers that add up to target. Return their indices.
twoSum.story: Guest book at a party. Check if your dance partner already signed in, if not, sign yourself.
code: public static int[] solve(int[] nums, int target) {
// STEP 2 — LOOP: Go through each guest at the party
// STEP 3 — CHECK: Has my dance partner already signed in?
// STEP 4 — STORE: Sign myself into the guest book

steps.desc: Init: Create empty HashMap (guest book). guestBook = {}.
Check guestBook for 7 → NOT found. No dance partner yet.
Store nums[0]=2 → index 0 in guestBook. guestBook = {2:0}.
Check guestBook for 2 → FOUND at index 0! Dance partner found! Return [0, 1].

quiz.q: Why do we store AFTER checking in Two Sum?
options: To prevent matching a number with itself (e.g., target=4, nums=[2,2] would wrongly match index 0 with index 0)
To save memory by only storing what's needed
quiz.explain: If we store first, then check, a single element could match itself as its own complement. By checking first, we ensure the complement was seen at a different index.

groupAnagrams.title: Group Anagrams
groupAnagrams.description: Group words that are anagrams of each other using sorted-key fingerprinting.
groupAnagrams.story: Filing cabinet. Sort each word to create a fingerprint (key). File the word into the matching drawer.
code: // STEP 1 — INIT: Create the filing cabinet (HashMap)
// STEP 2 — LOOP: Go through each word in the pile
// STEP 3 — FINGERPRINT: Sort the letters to create a unique key
// STEP 4 — STORE: File into the correct drawer
// STEP 5 — RETURN: Return all drawers as a list of lists

steps.desc: Init: Create empty HashMap (filing cabinet). groups = {}.
word="eat". Sort letters → fingerprint = "aet".
"aet" NOT in map → create new drawer. Add "eat". groups = {"aet": ["eat"]}.
word="tea". Sort letters → fingerprint = "aet".
"aet" ALREADY in map! Add "tea" to same drawer. groups = {"aet": ["eat", "tea"]}.
word="tan". Sort letters → fingerprint = "ant".
"ant" NOT in map → create new drawer. Add "tan". groups = {"aet": ["eat", "tea"], "ant": ["tan"]}.
word="ate". Sort letters → fingerprint = "aet".
"aet" ALREADY in map! Add "ate". Result: {"aet": ["eat", "tea", "ate"], "ant": ["tan"]}.

quiz.q: Why do we sort each word's characters before using it as a HashMap key?
options: Anagrams have the same characters in different order — sorting normalizes them to the same key
Sorting makes the words alphabetically ordered in the output
HashMap requires sorted keys to work correctly
quiz.explain: Anagrams contain the exact same characters, just rearranged. Sorting each word gives all anagrams the same 'fingerprint', allowing us to group them under one HashMap key.

validSudoku.title: Valid Sudoku
validSudoku.description: Determine if a 9×9 Sudoku board is valid using HashSets for rows, columns, and boxes.
validSudoku.story: Hotel check-in. 3 guest books (row, col, box). If a name appears in ANY book already → invalid!
code: // STEP 1 — INIT: Create 3 sets of guest books
// STEP 2 — LOOP: Check in each guest (cell)
// STEP 5 — CHECK: Duplicate in any guest book?
// STEP 6 — STORE: Register in all 3 books

steps.desc: Init: Create 3 arrays of 9 HashSets each — rows[], cols[], boxes[]. All empty.
Cell (0,0)='5'. boxIndex = (0/3)*3 + (0/3) = 0. Check rows[0], cols[0], boxes[0] — all empty. Add '5' to all three.
Cell (0,1)='3'. boxIndex = (0/3)*3 + (1/3) = 0. Check rows[0], cols[1], boxes[0] — no duplicate. Add '3' to all three.
Cell (0,2)='.'. Skip empty cell — continue to next.
Cell (0,4)='7'. boxIndex = (0/3)*3 + (4/3) = 1. Check rows[0], cols[4], boxes[1] — no duplicate. Add '7' to all three.

quiz.q: What is the formula to determine which 3×3 box a cell at (row, col) belongs to?
quiz.explain: Integer division row/3 gives the box row (0,1,2) and col/3 gives the box column. Multiplying box-row by 3 and adding box-column maps all 9 boxes to unique indices 0-8.

subarraySum.title: Subarray Sum Equals K
subarraySum.description: Count continuous subarrays whose sum equals K using prefix sum + HashMap.
subarraySum.story: Walking a road tracking distance. At each point, check: 'Was there a past milestone where the gap to here = K?'
code: public static int solve(int[] nums, int k) {
// STEP 1 — INIT: Create logbook of prefix sums
// STEP 4 — CHECK: Gap = runningSum - k. In logbook?

steps.desc: Init: Create logbook HashMap. Seed with {0: 1} — distance 0 happened once.
num=1, runningSum = 0+1 = 1. Check logbook for 1-2 = -1 → NOT found. count stays 0. Store: logbook = {0:1, 1:1}.
num=1, runningSum = 1+1 = 2. Check logbook for 2-2 = 0 → FOUND (1 time)! count = 0+1 = 1. Store: logbook = {0:1, 1:1, 2:1}.
num=1, runningSum = 2+1 = 3. Check logbook for 3-2 = 1 → FOUND (1 time)! count = 1+1 = 2. Store: logbook = {0:1, 1:1, 2:1, 3:1}.
Loop complete. Return count = 2. Two subarrays sum to K=2: [1,1] starting at index 0 and [1,1] starting at index 1.

quiz.q: Why must we seed the logbook with (0, 1) before starting the loop?
options: So that subarrays starting from index 0 whose sum equals K are counted (runningSum - K = 0 must exist)
HashMap requires at least one entry to function
quiz.explain: If the running sum at some index equals exactly K, then runningSum - K = 0. Without seeding 0 in the map, we'd miss subarrays that start at the very beginning of the array.
